metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify what the actual confusion source is: `_write_event_file` in `inbound_translate.py` does ONE file-write per call with NO git commit (not a 'batch commit'); correct the plan's 'What/Why' description and the `__main__.py` AC to reference the real inbound write mechanism before the documentation is drafted.
findings.finding: The plan's central premise — that the reconciler is 'the ONLY place in the tree that already batches many event-files into fewer commits' and has an 'inbound commit-batcher' — is refuted by the code. `inbound_translate._write_event_file` writes ONE event file per call using `os.replace` under the store lock but issues NO `git commit`. There is no inbound batch commit in the reconciler. The plan's rationale section and the scoped 'one-liner near the batch commit in `__main__.py`' AC both rest on this false claim.
findings.location: Plan § What/Why — 'the reconciler... is the ONLY place in the tree that already batches many event-files into fewer commits'; AC bullet for `__main__.py` — 'a one-liner near the batch commit'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'inbound commit-batcher' / 'batches many event-files into fewer commits' with an accurate description: the reconciler's inbound leaf-appliers call `_write_event_file` once per event (one lock-acquire + one `os.replace`, no git commit). The outbound path (`_apply_batch`) dispatches Jira REST mutations — also not a local store batch-commit. The `__main__.py` AC item should either be dropped or redirected to the only actual reconciler git-commit calls (binding-store commit in `reconcile.py`, legacy-lock purge in `__main__.py`) if those are what triggered agent confusion.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the signpost target from `__main__.py` to `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/applier.py` (where `_apply_batch` is defined), or explicitly justify what the 'batch commit' in `__main__.py` refers to and add a comment anchor there.
findings.finding: The plan targets `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/__main__.py` for 'a one-liner near the batch commit', but `__main__.py` contains no batch-commit logic. The `_apply_batch` function the plan's 'Why' section describes as the source of agent confusion (reconciler batching many event-files into fewer commits) lives in `applier.py`, not `__main__.py`. A grep for 'batch' in `__main__.py` returns zero hits; `__main__.py` is a pass orchestrator that calls `run_pass()` which calls `reconcile.reconcile_once()` which calls into `applier._apply_batch`.
findings.location: Scope section: 'a one-liner near the batch commit in `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/__main__.py`'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the AC and Scope bullet referencing `__main__.py` with `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/applier.py`, adding the one-liner signpost near the `_apply_batch` function definition (line 556) or its module docstring, since that is the symbol the plan's 'Why' section identifies as the trap.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify and correct the factual premise before writing the 'Two writers, one store' section: audit whether the reconciler inbound path (`_write_event_file`) or any reconciler module actually batches multiple event-file writes into fewer git commits (vs. one write-per-lock-acquire), and revise the 'Why' section and AC line 1 to accurately name the specific reconciler artifact (e.g., `applier._apply_batch` as an *outbound* Jira sequencer, not an inbound commit batcher) that causes the vocabulary confusion.
findings.finding: The plan's 'Why' section claims the reconciler 'is the ONLY place in the tree that already batches many event-files into fewer commits,' but the actual code does not support this: `inbound_translate._write_event_file` acquires the write lock and writes exactly ONE event file per call (no batching of git commits); `applier._apply_batch` is an outbound Jira mutation sequencer, not an inbound store commit batcher; and `compact.py` / `fsck.py` in `_commands` also perform multi-event batched git commits. This factual error will propagate directly into the 'Two writers, one store' section (AC line 1) if the implementer writes it from the plan's premise — producing a doc that mis-describes what the reconciler does and could deepen rather than resolve agent confusion.
findings.location: Why section / Scope paragraph 1 / AC line 1 ('flagging the reconciler inbound batcher as a Jira-sync internal')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the vague 'near the batch commit in `__main__.py`' placement instruction with a specific anchor (e.g., near the `run_pass()` call or the module docstring) and clarify that the actual inbound-write loop lives in `applier._apply_batch` / `apply_inbound_records.py`, so the signpost in `__main__.py` correctly points agents to the right sub-module.
findings.finding: The plan instructs adding a 'one-liner near the batch commit in `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/__main__.py`,' but `__main__.py` contains no batch commit — it is a pass orchestrator that calls `run_pass()` → `reconcile.reconcile_once()`. There is no 'batch commit' code point in this file to anchor the signpost to, so the implementer has no concrete placement target and may add the docstring in an arbitrary or misleading location.
findings.suggested_fix: Add the signpost comment to the `__main__.py` module docstring or to the `run_pass()` function docstring, noting that store writes happen in `applier.py` → `apply_inbound` → `inbound_translate._write_event_file` (not here), and cross-reference `docs/architecture.md#two-writers-one-store`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify and correct the plan's claim that `__main__.py` contains a 'batch commit' site for inbound event-files; if no such site exists (confirmed by code inspection), replace the `__main__.py` AC item with a locatable anchor (e.g., the `_purge_committed_reconciler_locks` commit or the `applier._apply_batch` outbound dispatcher), and correct the 'Why' section's premise that the reconciler 'batches many event-files into fewer commits' to match what the code actually does.
findings.finding: The plan's AC item 'a one-liner near the batch commit in `__main__.py`' references a 'batch commit' site in `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/__main__.py` that does not exist as described. The only `commit`-adjacent call in that file is `git_adapter.commit_tree` inside `_purge_committed_reconciler_locks` (a legacy lock-file migration, not an inbound event-file batch). The reconciler's inbound write path (`inbound_translate._write_event_file`) does `os.replace()` under the store lock but issues no `git commit`. An agent implementing this AC cannot locate the specified anchor, making the placement rule undefined.
findings.location: ## Acceptance Criteria — 'a one-liner near the batch commit in `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/__main__.py`'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'a one-liner near the batch commit in `__main__.py`' with a signpost at a real location — e.g., the module docstring of `applier.py` (which owns `_apply_batch`, the outbound Jira REST sequencer that agents confuse with a local write-batch) or `apply_inbound.py` (whose leaf appliers call `_write_event_file` one-at-a-time). Update the 'Why' prose to say 'the reconciler's `_apply_batch` is an OUTBOUND Jira REST sequencer, not a local event-file committer' rather than describing a non-existent inbound commit-batcher.
Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to the plan (Scope or a new sub-section) the intended disambiguation for each of the four vocabulary terms (reconcile / apply / batch / sync), specifying what each term means in the local-write-path context vs. the reconciler context, so an implementing agent can author the table correctly without guessing.
findings.finding: The plan names an 'overloaded vocabulary' table (reconcile / apply / batch / sync) as required content for the 'Two writers, one store' section but never specifies what disambiguation each term should carry. An agent authoring the section has no defined value-vocabulary to draw from — the correct disambiguations for 'batch' (Jira REST outbound sequencer vs. absent local batch-commit), 'apply' (inbound leaf applier vs. general event write), 'sync' (SYNC event type vs. Jira sync pass), and 'reconcile' (the pass itself vs. convergence) are not enumerated anywhere in the plan.
findings.location: ## Scope — 'listing the overloaded vocabulary (reconcile / apply / batch / sync)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC item (or Scope bullet) that either updates the 'WS-B3: batch-apply' wording in the cold-stall-chalk epic to reference the new 'Two writers, one store' section, or adds a deprecation/disambiguation note there, so the stale artifact no longer strands agents who encounter it before architecture.md.
findings.finding: The 'Why' section explicitly names the 'stale epic cold-stall-chalk' with 'WS-B3: batch-apply' wording as a root cause of agent confusion, but neither the Scope nor the Acceptance Criteria include updating, deprecating, or cross-referencing that stale artifact. After this plan lands, agents grepping the old epic will still encounter the misleading 'WS-B3: batch-apply' language and may not find the new disambiguating section.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Scope: 'Update (or annotate) the cold-stall-chalk epic's WS-B3 wording to cross-reference the new architecture.md section, marking it as Jira-sync-internal context only.' Add a corresponding AC: '[ ] cold-stall-chalk epic WS-B3 entry links to or notes the Two writers, one store section.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
